questions.text: Based on Keckley's stated values in the preface, how would she likely respond to Mrs. Lincoln's accusation against the messenger?
questions.held_out_passage: She turned to him fiercely: "So you are on guard to-night--on guard in the White House after helping to murder the President!" "Pardon me, but I did not help to murder the President. I could never stoop to murder--much less to the murder of so good and great a man as the President."

questions.text: Given Keckley's relationship with Mrs. Lincoln described in the training text, what role would she take during Mrs. Lincoln's grief?
questions.held_out_passage: She denied admittance to almost every one, and I was her only companion, except her children, in the days of her great sorrow.

questions.text: How would Keckley characterize her emotional response to witnessing Lincoln's death, based on her narrative style?
questions.held_out_passage: I gazed long at the face, and turned away with tears in my eyes and a choking sensation in my throat.

questions.text: Would Keckley judge Mrs. Lincoln harshly for her extreme grief and seclusion?
questions.held_out_passage: But far be it from me to harshly judge the sorrow of any one.

questions.text: How would Keckley respond to Mrs. Lincoln's unfounded accusations, given her character?
questions.held_out_passage: I bathed Mrs. Lincoln's head with cold water, and soothed the terrible tornado as best I could.

questions.text: Based on Keckley's preface about balance and fairness, would she present only negative aspects of the people around her?
questions.held_out_passage: If I have portrayed the dark side of slavery, I also have painted the bright side.

questions.text: What would be Keckley's likely emotional state while witnessing Mrs. Lincoln's extreme grief?

questions.text: How would Keckley handle the messenger's trembling denial of guilt?

questions.text: Would Keckley believe the messenger was truly guilty based on her approach to truth?
questions.held_out_passage: "No, no! don't say that," he broke in. "God knows that I am innocent."

questions.text: How would Keckley describe the atmosphere in the White House after Lincoln's death?
questions.held_out_passage: Every room in the White House was darkened, and every one spoke in subdued tones, and moved about with muffled tread. The very atmosphere breathed of the great sorrow which weighed heavily upon each heart.

questions.text: Based on her preface, would Keckley exaggerate the details of Lincoln's death?
questions.held_out_passage: much has been omitted, but nothing has been exaggerated.

questions.text: How would Keckley characterize the universal response to Lincoln's death?
questions.held_out_passage: Ah! never was man so widely mourned before. The whole world bowed their heads in grief when Abraham Lincoln died.

questions.text: Would Keckley show compassion to those who failed in their duties, like the messenger?
questions.held_out_passage: "I did wrong, I admit, and I have bitterly repented it, but I did not help to kill the President."

questions.text: How would Keckley respond to Robert Lincoln's behavior during his mother's crisis?
questions.held_out_passage: Robert was very tender to his mother in the days of her sorrow. He suffered deeply, as his haggard face indicated, but he was ever manly and collected when in the presence of his mother.

questions.text: Would Keckley judge Mrs. Lincoln's refusal to see visitors as unreasonable?
questions.held_out_passage: Mrs. Lincoln was extremely nervous, and she refused to have anybody about her but myself. Many ladies called, but she received none of them.

questions.text: How would Keckley interpret young Tad's attempts to comfort his mother?
questions.held_out_passage: Sometimes he would throw his arms around her neck, and exclaim, between his broken sobs, "Don't cry so, Mamma! don't cry, or you will make me cry, too! You will break my heart."

questions.text: Would Keckley believe the messenger's explanation about being distracted by the play?
questions.held_out_passage: "I was attracted by the play, and did not see the assassin enter the box."

questions.text: How would Keckley characterize Lincoln's appearance in death?

questions.text: Would Keckley support Mrs. Lincoln's theory about the messenger's involvement in the assassination?

questions.text: How would Keckley view the consequences of Mrs. Lincoln's extreme seclusion during grief?
